accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' + Section 4.3 cure period asymmetry (30 days Customer, 60 days Lattice) + absence of termination for convenience
accepted_findings.external_comment: The combination of no termination-for-convenience right, 100% remaining-fees obligation on any non-breach termination, asymmetric cure periods heavily favoring Provider, and automatic renewal with only 60-day opt-out creates structural lock-in. On a three-year $3M Initial Term, this becomes a non-dischargeable obligation with no practical exit except Provider's material breach—which Provider gets 60 days to cure while Customer gets only 30.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: A Customer in Year 1 of a 3-year term cannot exit without either proving Provider material breach AND waiting 60 days for cure attempt while paying fees OR paying 100% of remaining fees. This eliminates all strategic flexibility: acquisition, technology migration, budget reduction, regulatory change—none permit exit. The auto-renewal extends this trap indefinitely unless Customer opts out 60 days before each renewal.
accepted_findings.position: Customer must have termination for convenience after Year 1 with nominal wind-down fee (3 months or less), OR early termination fee capped at 25-50% of remaining fees with immediate effect, OR mutual 45-day cure periods with clear materiality standard.
accepted_findings.fallback: Termination for convenience after 18 months; 50% early termination fee; mutual 45-day cure.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any structure requiring 100% remaining fees on convenience termination in a multi-year auto-renewing deal with asymmetric cure is existential. Customer cannot accept economic lock-in of this magnitude.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Courts will enforce termination-fee provisions but disfavor penalty clauses and may refuse enforcement where fee + lock-in structure eliminates all practical remedy.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 carve-out (A) 'AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM' + Section 8.6 carve-out (B) unlimited confidentiality breach liability + Section 5.3 Security Incident definition + Section 7.1 Confidential Information includes Customer Data
accepted_findings.external_comment: Carve-out (A) 'amounts owed' plus carve-out (B) unlimited confidentiality breach creates a structure where any material claim can be recharacterized to escape the 12-month cap. If Lattice suffers a Security Incident exposing Customer Data, the claim could be framed as either confidentiality breach (unlimited under carve-out B) or 'amounts owed' for refund/credit (unlimited under carve-out A). The 12-month cap becomes illusory—nearly every material claim has a path around it.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Any payment dispute becomes 'amounts owed' (unlimited). Any data breach becomes confidentiality breach (unlimited). Any warranty claim can be recast as refund demand (amounts owed, unlimited). Combined with cyber insurance at only $5M, Lattice faces unlimited exposure beyond insurance on every Security Incident, and Customer faces unlimited exposure on every fee dispute recharacterized as 'amounts owed.' The risk allocation framework has no effective cap.
accepted_findings.position: Remove carve-out (A) entirely or limit to undisputed unpaid invoices for services actually rendered. Cap carve-out (B) confidentiality breach at $10M super-cap. Add gross negligence/willful misconduct/fraud carve-out as separate item.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap carve-out (A) at 24 months of fees; cap carve-out (B) at $15M; or cap both at 3x the general cap.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Unlimited liability on 'amounts owed' for any fee dispute AND unlimited liability for any confidentiality breach simultaneously is existential. At least one must be capped or the general cap becomes meaningless.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Liability caps are enforceable but must actually limit liability; carve-outs that swallow the cap may be recharacterized as illusory and unenforceable.

accepted_findings.source_text: Upon any expiration or termination of this Agreement: (a) Customer's right to access and use the Subscription Services will immediately terminate; (b) Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination, and, if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term;
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Upon any expiration or termination of this Agreement: (a) Customer's right to access and use the Subscription Services will immediately terminate; (b) Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination; provided, however, that if Customer terminates this Agreement for convenience (other than pursuant to Section 4.3), Customer shall pay Lattice, in lieu of all remaining Fees under the then-current Subscription Term, a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term;
accepted_findings.external_comment: We propose to cap liability upon a termination for convenience at 50% of the remaining Fees, rather than 100%. This recognizes that Provider avoids future delivery and support costs when the contract terminates early.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Current language requires Customer to pay 100% of all remaining Fees if Customer terminates for any reason other than Provider's material breach, even if Customer terminates due to business closure, bankruptcy, or a change in business needs unrelated to Provider's performance. On a three-year $3M deal terminated after year one, Customer owes the full $2M balance despite Provider incurring zero future hosting, support, or delivery costs. A 50% termination fee compensates Provider for lost margin while avoiding windfall.
accepted_findings.position: Termination fee capped at 50% of remaining Fees upon convenience termination by Customer; Provider's costs avoided justify the reduction.
accepted_findings.fallback: Termination fee at 75% of remaining Fees; or termination fee capped at 50% but applies only after the first year of the Initial Term; or termination fee steps down over time (75% in year 1, 50% in year 2, 25% in year 3).
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any construct requiring Customer to pay 100% of remaining Fees upon convenience termination in a multi-year deal exceeding $1M total value.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware courts enforce liquidated-damages clauses if reasonable and not punitive; 100% of remaining Fees with zero future costs may be challenged as a penalty.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Upon any expiration or termination of this Agreement: (a) Customer's right to access and use the Subscription Services will immediately terminate; (b) Customer will pay Lattice all Fees due and payable as of the effective date of termination; provided, however, that if Customer terminates this Agreement for convenience (other than pursuant to Section 4.3), Customer shall pay Lattice, in lieu of all remaining Fees under the then-current Subscription Term, a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term;

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by providing Customer with written notice (which may be by email) at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by not more than the greater of (a) five percent (5%) or (b) the percentage change in the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) published by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics over the twelve-month period ending sixty (60) days prior to the start of the applicable Renewal Term, upon providing Customer with written notice at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Any increase exceeding the foregoing cap requires Customer's written consent.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to cap annual price increases to protect against unconstrained escalation over multi-year renewals. The proposed cap tracks either a modest fixed percentage or inflation, whichever is greater.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: As drafted, Provider may increase Fees by any amount at each renewal with only 60 days' notice. Over a five-year relationship with automatic renewals, this creates unbounded exposure—Provider could double Fees year-over-year. Customer cannot budget or forecast accurately.
accepted_findings.position: Cap annual Fees increases at the greater of 5% or CPI-U over the prior twelve months; any increase exceeding the cap requires Customer's written consent.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap at 7% per year; or cap at CPI-U plus 3%; or require 90 days' notice for increases exceeding the cap.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Any construct allowing Provider to increase Fees by more than 10% per year without Customer's consent.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. No statutory price-cap rules apply to commercial SaaS contracts in Delaware.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may increase the Fees applicable to a Renewal Term by not more than the greater of (a) five percent (5%) or (b) the percentage change in the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) published by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics over the twelve-month period ending sixty (60) days prior to the start of the applicable Renewal Term, upon providing Customer with written notice at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term. Any such increase will take effect at the start of the next Renewal Term. Any increase exceeding the foregoing cap requires Customer's written consent.

accepted_findings.source_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term
accepted_findings.proposed_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, or three (3) months of Fees, whichever is less
accepted_findings.external_comment: Full payment for the remaining term following Customer termination effectively eliminates any practical termination right. A capped termination fee preserves recovery of reasonable costs and lost margin while affording Customer a genuine exit right in circumstances such as technology obsolescence, internal budget reallocation, or strategic pivot.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a termination-for-convenience right, Customer has no exit absent Provider's material breach. Current language makes Customer liable for 100% of remaining Fees even if terminating for legitimate business reasons. In a multi-year Initial Term scenario, Customer could be locked into 36 months of payment obligations regardless of business utility. This effectively transforms a subscription into secured debt without the corresponding legal structure or pricing discount.
accepted_findings.position: Termination for convenience with 90-day notice; no termination fee after Year 1; 25% termination fee (or 2 months' Fees, whichever is less) if terminated in Year 1.
accepted_findings.fallback: 50% termination fee or 3 months' Fees, whichever is less.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Full payment of remaining Fees without any cap or discount is a blocker when combined with lack of termination for convenience. Customer must have a realistic exit mechanism.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware courts will enforce termination fees but disfavor liquidated damages that function as penalties. Full payment of remaining term with no discount for avoided costs approaches penalty territory.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay a termination fee equal to fifty percent (50%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, or three (3) months of Fees, whichever is less

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 4.3A Termination for Convenience. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form for any reason or no reason upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to Lattice. In the event of such termination, Customer shall pay (a) all Fees due and payable through the effective date of termination, and (b) if termination occurs within the first twelve (12) months of the Initial Term, a termination fee equal to twenty-five percent (25%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, or two (2) months of Fees under the applicable Order Form, whichever is less. No termination fee is due if termination occurs after the first twelve (12) months of the Initial Term.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement currently provides no termination for convenience, leaving Customer bound for the full Subscription Term regardless of changed business circumstances. A termination-for-convenience right with reasonable wind-down notice and a modest early-termination fee (applicable only in Year 1) appropriately balances Customer's need for strategic flexibility with Provider's interest in revenue predictability and cost recovery during the initial onboarding period.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without this right and combined with the 100% fee obligation, Customer is locked into potentially 36 months of non-dischargeable payment obligations even if the Subscription Services become obsolete or redundant. This is not a subscription model—it is a long-term financing agreement disguised as SaaS.
accepted_findings.position: Termination for convenience with 90-day notice and no termination fee after Year 1.
accepted_findings.fallback: Termination for convenience with 90-day notice; 25% termination fee (or 2 months' Fees, whichever is less) if terminated within first 18 months; no fee thereafter.
accepted_findings.walkaway: No termination-for-convenience right combined with full payment of remaining Fees is a blocker. Customer must have a genuine exit path.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware law permits termination for convenience in service agreements and does not disfavor such clauses.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 4.3A Termination for Convenience. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form for any reason or no reason upon ninety (90) days' prior written notice to Lattice. In the event of such termination, Customer shall pay (a) all Fees due and payable through the effective date of termination, and (b) if termination occurs within the first twelve (12) months of the Initial Term, a termination fee equal to twenty-five percent (25%) of the Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term, or two (2) months of Fees under the applicable Order Form, whichever is less. No termination fee is due if termination occurs after the first twelve (12) months of the Initial Term.

accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)
accepted_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY), AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current carve-out (A) for 'amounts owed under any Order Form' creates unlimited liability for any payment dispute and effectively eliminates the cap in fee-related disputes. Additionally, limiting the cap to contractual and negligence-based claims while excluding gross negligence and willful misconduct is inconsistent with industry standards and creates uninsurable tail risk.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Carve-out (A) means any fee dispute—even a good-faith disagreement over interpretation of usage tiers or overage charges—exposes both parties to unlimited liability beyond the 12-month cap. For Provider, a Customer claim for 'amounts owed' (e.g., refund demand based on alleged service failures) could exceed all fees ever paid. The absence of a gross negligence/willful misconduct carve-out exposes both parties to claims that their insurance will not cover.
accepted_findings.position: Remove 'amounts owed' carve-out entirely; add standard gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud carve-out; add customer indemnity carve-out to match IP indemnity carve-out structure.
accepted_findings.fallback: Narrow 'amounts owed' to unpaid invoices for services actually rendered (excluding any refund or credit claims); add gross negligence and willful misconduct carve-out only.
accepted_findings.walkaway: Carve-out (A) must not apply to disputed fees, refund claims, or damages claims recharacterized as 'amounts owed.' If amounts-owed carve-out remains, it applies only to undisputed invoices for services actually delivered.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware public policy supports narrowly construed liability caps and does not favor unlimited liability for ordinary payment disputes.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY), AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD

accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)
accepted_findings.proposed_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), UP TO AN AGGREGATE CAP OF TEN MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($10,000,000); (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY); (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY); AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD
accepted_findings.external_comment: Current language imposes unlimited liability for any breach of confidentiality obligations, which include all Customer Data. Given that Security Incidents involving Customer Data are addressed in Section 5.3 and would constitute confidentiality breaches, this creates uncapped exposure for data breaches. A reasonable super-cap tied to available cyber insurance limits balances the parties' interests.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Provider's cyber insurance is capped at $5M. An uncapped confidentiality breach obligation means any significant Security Incident—regardless of degree of fault—creates liability exposure of 2x or more above available insurance coverage. For a SaaS provider handling customer data at scale, this creates uninsurable existential risk on every contract.
accepted_findings.position: Super-cap for confidentiality breaches at $10M (2x cyber insurance limits), with remainder of confidentiality breaches subject to general cap.
accepted_findings.fallback: Super-cap at $15M; or super-cap applies only to Security Incidents as defined in Section 5.3, with other confidentiality breaches subject to general cap.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Unlimited liability for data breach creates exposure materially exceeding available insurance and is not required by Delaware law for commercial SaaS contracts absent special circumstances.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), UP TO AN AGGREGATE CAP OF TEN MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($10,000,000); (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY); (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY); AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 10.2 mandatory arbitration in San Francisco + Section 10.2 jury waiver + class action waiver + Section 8.7 consequential damages waiver including 'lost or corrupted data' + Section 2.3 SLA credits as sole remedy + Section 4.4(b) 100% remaining fees on termination
accepted_findings.external_comment: The waiver stack (arbitration + jury + class + consequential damages + SLA sole remedy + no data-loss recovery) eliminates all practical remedies for sub-$100K claims, which are the majority of SaaS disputes. A Customer suffering $50K in losses from service outages must arbitrate in San Francisco (cost: $30K-80K), cannot aggregate with other customers, cannot recover consequential damages including lost business or corrupted data, is limited to SLA credits if service-related, and cannot terminate without paying 100% remaining fees. The remedy costs exceed the claim value, creating practical immunity for all claims under $100K.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The combination of procedural barriers (San Francisco arbitration, class waiver) plus substantive remedy limitations (consequential damages waiver, SLA sole remedy, termination fee) plus lock-in creates three tiers of claim where Customer has no economic remedy: (1) claims under $50K are uneconomical to pursue in arbitration, (2) claims $50K-$200K face consequential damages waiver plus sole remedy limitations that cap recovery below arbitration costs, (3) claims over $200K face liability cap that may be less than damages plus arbitration costs on large deals.
accepted_findings.position: Remove class waiver entirely; arbitration in Customer's home state or remote; remove 'lost or corrupted data' from consequential damages waiver; add exception to sole remedy for repeated SLA failures; cap termination fee at 25-50%.
accepted_findings.fallback: Retain class waiver but add small-claims court carve-out for claims under $25K; arbitration in defendant's state; remove 'lost or corrupted data' from consequential waiver; cap termination fee at 50%.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware choice of law; AAA arbitration rules; federal FAA governs arbitration enforceability.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The Agreement currently provides Customer with 30 days to cure a material breach but requires Customer to wait 60 days before terminating for Provider's breach. Mutual 60-day cure periods better reflect parity between sophisticated commercial parties and provide both sides adequate time to investigate and remedy alleged breaches.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Asymmetric cure periods create operational imbalance: Customer faces immediate termination risk for payment delays or inadvertent technical violations after only 30 days, while Provider retains double that period to cure performance failures. In a subscription context where service degradation can compound over weeks, a 30-day window exposes Customer to prolonged service deficiencies without remedy while Provider enjoys extended latitude.
accepted_findings.position: Mutual 60-day cure periods for both Parties.
accepted_findings.fallback: Mutual 45-day cure periods; or retain 60-day cure for Provider but increase Customer cure to 45 days.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Delaware law permits cure period negotiation and does not mandate asymmetric treatment.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.

accepted_findings.category: SLA sole remedy vs repeated failures
accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement, and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Lattice fails to meet the applicable service-level commitment in any three (3) months during any consecutive six (6) month period, Customer may, in addition to any accrued service credits, terminate the affected Subscription Service upon thirty (30) days' written notice without liability for future Fees under the affected Order Form.
accepted_findings.external_comment: The current language limits termination rights for repeated SLA failures to the general material-breach process, which requires a 60-day cure period. This creates an asymmetry where persistent underperformance—even if chronic but individually curable—does not provide an adequate exit mechanism. We propose a specific threshold (three monthly failures in six months) that triggers immediate termination rights with appropriate notice.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: In a SaaS relationship where Customer depends on availability for business operations, chronic underperformance can degrade Customer's own service to end users. The current sole-remedy framing combined with Section 4.3's 60-day cure effectively traps Customer in a poorly performing service. The proposed three-in-six threshold allows termination after a pattern emerges, without forfeiting prepaid fees for the remainder of a multi-year term.
accepted_findings.position: Add exception to sole-remedy language permitting termination for repeated SLA failures (three monthly failures in six months) with 30-day notice and without liability for future fees.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add exception permitting termination for repeated SLA failures (four monthly failures in any twelve-month period) with 60-day notice and pro-rata refund of prepaid fees for the period post-termination.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Standard SaaS sole-remedy and cure-period analysis under UCC Article 2 analogies and Delaware contract law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if Lattice fails to meet the applicable service-level commitment in any three (3) months during any consecutive six (6) month period, Customer may, in addition to any accrued service credits, terminate the affected Subscription Service upon thirty (30) days' written notice without liability for future Fees under the affected Order Form.

accepted_findings.source_text: Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that (a) no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term, and (b) Lattice will provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any modification that materially changes the user interface, data structure, integration methods, or security architecture. If any such modification materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Service upon written notice within thirty (30) days after the effective date of the modification and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid Fees for the period after termination.
accepted_findings.external_comment: While we acknowledge Provider's need to evolve the platform, the current unilateral modification right creates uncertainty in Customer's operational planning, particularly where integrations, workflows, or compliance controls are built on specific functionality. We propose notice and termination rights for material adverse changes, consistent with industry-standard SaaS terms.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Customer may build significant technical integrations, train staff, or implement compliance processes around the Subscription Services as provided at contract signature. Unilateral modification with only a 'no material diminishment' standard—which Provider interprets—creates risk that functionality Customer depends on could be removed, redesigned, or moved behind a higher pricing tier mid-term. The proposed notice and termination right limits Customer's downside to a pro-rata refund rather than being locked into an unusable service for the remainder of a multi-year term.
accepted_findings.position: Add 30-day advance notice requirement for material changes to UI, data structure, integrations, or security; add termination-for-convenience right within 30 days of change effective date with pro-rata refund.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add 15-day advance notice for material changes; termination right exercisable within 15 days of change with pro-rata refund; narrow 'material change' to changes that break documented API integrations or eliminate features listed in Order Form.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Unilateral modification provisions are enforceable if reasonable notice and exit mechanisms are provided.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that (a) no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term, and (b) Lattice will provide Customer with at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice of any modification that materially changes the user interface, data structure, integration methods, or security architecture. If any such modification materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Customer may terminate the affected Subscription Service upon written notice within thirty (30) days after the effective date of the modification and receive a pro-rata refund of prepaid Fees for the period after termination.

accepted_findings.source_text: The arbitration will be conducted in San Francisco, California by a single arbitrator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.proposed_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the state where Customer's principal place of business is located, or if both Parties agree, via remote proceedings, by a single arbitator selected in accordance with such rules.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Requiring Customer to arbitrate all disputes in San Francisco creates a geographic and cost burden if Customer is not located in California. Situs in Customer's home state (or remote arbitration by mutual consent) eliminates unnecessary travel costs and counsel coordination expenses, particularly for mid-value disputes where arbitration costs can exceed the amount in controversy.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Exclusive San Francisco arbitration situs imposes asymmetric costs on Customer if Customer is not California-based. For disputes under $100K, cross-country arbitration costs (travel, local counsel coordination, time away from business) can exceed recovery, effectively denying Customer a practical remedy. Provider's principal place of business is San Francisco; requiring Customer to come to Provider's home jurisdiction for all disputes creates home-field advantage and economic deterrent to enforcement.
accepted_findings.position: Arbitration in Customer's principal place of business, or remote proceedings by mutual agreement.
accepted_findings.fallback: Arbitration in the state of the defendant's principal place of business (floating venue).
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware choice of law per Section 10.1, but arbitration situs is procedural and set by agreement. AAA rules permit remote arbitration.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: The arbitration will be conducted in the state where Customer's principal place of business is located, or if both Parties agree, via remote proceedings, by a single arbitator selected in accordance with such rules.

accepted_findings.source_text: EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)
accepted_findings.external_comment: The IP indemnity carve-out exposes Provider to unlimited liability for third-party IP claims without any cap. While IP indemnity carve-outs are common in enterprise software agreements, they are typically paired with contractual or insurance-based super-caps to manage tail risk. The current structure creates uninsurable exposure, particularly given the breadth of the indemnity (covers patents, copyrights, trademarks, and trade secrets with no knowledge qualifier or registration requirement). Consider adding a super-cap at 2–3x the general liability cap or at available insurance limits.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: A well-funded patent troll or competitor could assert claims exceeding Provider's total enterprise value. The indemnity covers 'any damages finally awarded' with no cap, meaning a single patent infringement judgment could bankrupt the company. Provider's E&O insurance ($5M) will not cover judgments or settlements exceeding that amount, leaving the delta uninsured.
accepted_findings.position: Cap IP indemnity at $15M (3x general cap) or $10M (2x general cap), whichever provides sufficient headroom above likely claim size for this deal.
accepted_findings.fallback: Cap IP indemnity at available E&O insurance limits ($5M) plus an additional layer of $5M, for total super-cap of $10M.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Unlimited IP indemnity obligations are enforceable but create material uninsurable risk.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.6 Disputed Invoices. Customer may withhold payment of the specific disputed amount of any invoice, provided that Customer (a) pays all undisputed portions of the invoice by the due date, (b) provides Lattice with written notice of the disputed amount and a reasonably detailed description of the basis for the dispute within fifteen (15) days of receiving the invoice, and (c) cooperates in good faith to resolve the dispute. Any withholding under this Section 3.6 shall not constitute a breach or default, and no late fees or interest shall accrue on the disputed amount during the pendency of a good-faith dispute. If the dispute is resolved in Lattice's favor, Customer shall pay the disputed amount within ten (10) days of resolution, together with interest at the rate specified in Section 3.3 from the original due date.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to clarify the process for handling invoice disputes, so that good-faith disputes don't trigger late fees or suspension while we work together to resolve them.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Without a dispute-withholding provision, late fees and suspension rights apply mechanically to any unpaid amount, even when Customer disputes the invoice in good faith. This forces Customer to choose between paying contested charges or risking 1.5% monthly interest and service suspension within 40 days. A $500K annual contract with a disputed $50K line item could trigger suspension over an accounting disagreement.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may withhold the specific disputed amount; notice within 15 days; no late fees or suspension during good-faith dispute; undisputed amounts remain on standard terms.
accepted_findings.fallback: Notice within 10 days; or Customer pays into escrow during dispute; or late fees accrue but suspension is stayed during dispute.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. No statutory implied right to withhold disputed amounts in Delaware commercial law.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.6 Disputed Invoices. Customer may withhold payment of the specific disputed amount of any invoice, provided that Customer (a) pays all undisputed portions of the invoice by the due date, (b) provides Lattice with written notice of the disputed amount and a reasonably detailed description of the basis for the dispute within fifteen (15) days of receiving the invoice, and (c) cooperates in good faith to resolve the dispute. Any withholding under this Section 3.6 shall not constitute a breach or default, and no late fees or interest shall accrue on the disputed amount during the pendency of a good-faith dispute. If the dispute is resolved in Lattice's favor, Customer shall pay the disputed amount within ten (10) days of resolution, together with interest at the rate specified in Section 3.3 from the original due date.

accepted_findings.proposed_text: 3.7 Audit Rights. Upon reasonable advance written notice (not less than fifteen (15) business days), Customer may, at its own expense and no more than once per calendar year, audit Lattice's records directly relating to the calculation of Fees under this Agreement, including usage logs, seat counts, and any other data used to generate invoices. Such audit may be conducted during Lattice's normal business hours by Customer's employees or by an independent third-party auditor mutually acceptable to the Parties and bound by confidentiality obligations no less protective than those in Section 7. Lattice will provide reasonable cooperation and access to relevant records. If any audit reveals an overpayment by Customer of five percent (5%) or more of the Fees paid during the audited period, Lattice will reimburse Customer for the overpayment and the reasonable costs of the audit within thirty (30) days.
accepted_findings.external_comment: We'd like to include an audit right so Customer can periodically verify billing accuracy, particularly given the annual-in-advance invoicing model. This is standard in enterprise SaaS agreements and protects both parties by ensuring transparency.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: Annual advance invoicing could exceed $1M per year. Without audit rights, Customer has no contractual mechanism to verify usage-based charges, seat counts, or other billing inputs. If Provider's metering or billing systems contain errors—even inadvertent ones—Customer has no recourse short of litigation.
accepted_findings.position: Customer may audit once per year on 15 days' notice; Provider bears audit costs if overpayment exceeds 5%; reimbursement within 30 days.
accepted_findings.fallback: Audit rights limited to usage-based Fees (exclude fixed subscription Fees); or audit frequency capped at once every 18 months; or Provider bears costs only if overpayment exceeds 10%.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. No implied audit right under Delaware commercial law for fixed-price or usage-based SaaS contracts.
accepted_findings.suggested_text: 3.7 Audit Rights. Upon reasonable advance written notice (not less than fifteen (15) business days), Customer may, at its own expense and no more than once per calendar year, audit Lattice's records directly relating to the calculation of Fees under this Agreement, including usage logs, seat counts, and any other data used to generate invoices. Such audit may be conducted during Lattice's normal business hours by Customer's employees or by an independent third-party auditor mutually acceptable to the Parties and bound by confidentiality obligations no less protective than those in Section 7. Lattice will provide reasonable cooperation and access to relevant records. If any audit reveals an overpayment by Customer of five percent (5%) or more of the Fees paid during the audited period, Lattice will reimburse Customer for the overpayment and the reasonable costs of the audit within thirty (30) days.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement, and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.'
accepted_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes to add Section 2.3 language permitting termination for repeated SLA failures (three failures in six months) with 30-day notice and 'without liability for future Fees under the affected Order Form.' However, Section 4.4(b) remains unmodified and still requires Customer to 'pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' if Customer terminates other than for Lattice's uncured material breach under Section 4.3. The proposed SLA-based termination right in performance-obligations-analyst-001 is not a Section 4.3 termination for material breach with 60-day cure—it is a new termination mechanism with a three-in-six threshold. As drafted, the two provisions contradict: the new SLA termination right says 'without liability for future Fees' while Section 4.4(b) says Customer pays 100% of remaining Fees for any non-Section-4.3 termination. This creates ambiguity that will be exploited in litigation.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer terminates under the proposed performance-obligations-analyst-001 SLA repeated-failure provision, Provider will argue Section 4.4(b) still applies because the termination was not 'for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3'—it was for repeated SLA failures under a new Section 2.3 carve-out. Customer will argue the 'without liability for future Fees' language in the new SLA provision controls as lex specialis. The contract as signed contains an internal contradiction that makes the new SLA termination right economically unusable if Provider can still invoke Section 4.4(b). This defeats the entire purpose of the accepted finding. The contradiction must be resolved by conforming Section 4.4(b) to exclude SLA-based terminations or by adding explicit cross-reference language.
accepted_findings.position: Modify Section 4.4(b) to add carve-out: 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3 or pursuant to Section 2.3 for repeated SLA failures, Customer shall pay...' OR add to performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposed text: 'For clarity, termination under this paragraph shall not trigger the payment obligations in Section 4.4(b).'
accepted_findings.fallback: Add explicit cross-reference in Section 2.3 new language: 'without liability for future Fees under the affected Order Form, and Section 4.4(b) shall not apply to terminations under this paragraph.'
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Contract interpretation rules favor harmonizing provisions where possible, but where two provisions directly contradict on payment obligation, the ambiguity will be construed against the drafter (Provider), likely in Customer's favor. However, litigation risk and cost make this an unacceptable outcome for both parties.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' AND accepted findings commercial-terms-analyst-006, termination-remedies-analyst-002, termination-remedies-analyst-003 which all modify or interact with Section 4.4(b).
accepted_findings.external_comment: Three accepted findings propose edits to or additions interacting with Section 4.4(b)'s termination-fee structure: commercial-terms-analyst-006 replaces the 100% fee obligation with 50%; termination-remedies-analyst-002 replaces it with 50% or 3 months' Fees whichever is less; termination-remedies-analyst-003 inserts a new Section 4.3A termination-for-convenience right with 25% fee or 2 months' Fees (whichever is less) if terminated in first 12 months, zero fee after 12 months. These three findings propose THREE DIFFERENT termination-fee structures for what appears to be the same triggering event (Customer convenience termination). The contract as signed will contain contradictory fee obligations unless the findings are consolidated or one is designated as controlling.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: If all three edits are accepted as written, the signed contract will contain: (1) Section 4.4(b) saying 50% of remaining Fees (commercial-terms-analyst-006), (2) the same Section 4.4(b) also saying 50% or 3 months whichever is less (termination-remedies-analyst-002), and (3) a new Section 4.3A saying 25% or 2 months (whichever is less) if in first 12 months and zero fee thereafter (termination-remedies-analyst-003). Provider will argue the highest fee applies; Customer will argue the lowest. The three provisions are internally contradictory and cannot all be applied simultaneously. This is a redlining error that makes the contract unexecutable as drafted.
accepted_findings.position: Consolidate the three findings into a single termination-fee structure. Recommend adopting termination-remedies-analyst-003 as the most customer-favorable and coherent approach: new Section 4.3A with 25% or 2 months (whichever less) in Year 1, zero thereafter. Delete or mark as superseded commercial-terms-analyst-006 and termination-remedies-analyst-002. If Client insists on retaining fallback options, designate one finding as 'Position' and others as 'Fallback' or 'Alternative,' not as simultaneous edits.
accepted_findings.fallback: Retain termination-remedies-analyst-003 (new Section 4.3A) and modify Section 4.4(b) per termination-remedies-analyst-002 to apply only if Section 4.3A does not apply (i.e., as a backstop). Delete commercial-terms-analyst-006 as duplicative.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Three contradictory fee provisions governing the same event will render the contract ambiguous and likely unenforceable as to the fee term, forcing either settlement negotiation or judicial interpretation against the drafter.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap carve-outs as proposed by risk-allocation-analyst-002 and risk-allocation-analyst-003, which modify the same sentence with overlapping but non-identical language.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-002 proposes to replace Section 8.6 carve-outs with: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY), AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD'. Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-003 proposes to replace the same sentence with: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), UP TO AN AGGREGATE CAP OF TEN MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($10,000,000); (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY); (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY); AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD'. Both findings target the exact same source_text and propose replacement language. The difference is that risk-allocation-analyst-003 adds a $10M super-cap to the confidentiality carve-out while risk-allocation-analyst-002 leaves it uncapped. These are two alternative proposals for the same edit, not two separate edits. As presented, both cannot be applied—only one replacement can be made.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The redline will show two competing replacement texts for Section 8.6 carve-outs, which is nonsensical. The reviewer must choose between uncapped confidentiality breach liability (risk-allocation-analyst-002) or $10M super-cap (risk-allocation-analyst-003). The findings should have been structured as Position/Fallback within a single finding, not as two separate 'replace' markups targeting the same text. This creates ambiguity in the review process and risks the wrong version being accepted if not caught during QA.
accepted_findings.position: Consolidate risk-allocation-analyst-002 and risk-allocation-analyst-003 into a single finding. Adopt risk-allocation-analyst-003 as the Position (includes $10M super-cap on confidentiality breach) and note risk-allocation-analyst-002 as the Fallback (uncapped confidentiality but with gross negligence carve-out). The $10M super-cap is more commercially reasonable and insurable given Provider's $5M cyber insurance.
accepted_findings.fallback: Present both to Client as alternatives with clear recommendation for risk-allocation-analyst-003 due to insurability and coherence with Section 9.2 insurance limits.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. This is a drafting coherence issue, not a legal-interpretation issue.

accepted_findings.source_text: Section 2.4: 'Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term.' AND accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-002 which adds termination right for material adverse changes.
accepted_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-002 proposes to add language to Section 2.4 giving Customer a termination right with pro-rata refund if a modification 'materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services.' However, the existing Section 2.4 language says 'no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality.' These two standards—'materially diminish core functionality' (Provider's obligation) versus 'materially and adversely affects Customer's use' (Customer's termination trigger)—are overlapping but not identical. A modification could materially and adversely affect Customer's specific use case (e.g., breaks a critical integration Customer relies on) without materially diminishing the core functionality described in the Order Form (e.g., the platform still does the core job for most users). This creates ambiguity: does Customer have a termination right in that scenario or not? Provider will argue no (core functionality not diminished), Customer will argue yes (adverse effect on use). The two standards need to be harmonized or the relationship between them clarified.
accepted_findings.materiality_rationale: The accepted finding creates a termination right tied to 'material adverse effect on Customer's use' but leaves the existing 'no material diminishment of core functionality' standard in place. These are two different tests applied to the same modification event. If Provider makes a change that satisfies the 'no material diminishment' standard (Provider's view: core functionality intact) but Customer claims 'material adverse effect on use' (Customer's view: my integration broke), the contract does not clearly resolve who is right. The termination right becomes uncertain and likely leads to dispute. The coherence issue is that the new language in performance-obligations-analyst-002 does not integrate with or supersede the existing Section 2.4 standard—it adds a second, overlapping standard without clarifying the relationship.
accepted_findings.position: Modify the proposed performance-obligations-analyst-002 language to clarify: 'If any such modification (a) materially diminishes the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form, or (b) materially and adversely affects Customer's ability to use the Subscription Services for the purposes described in the applicable Order Form, Customer may terminate...' This makes clear the termination right applies to EITHER Provider breaching the no-diminishment standard OR a customer-specific material adverse impact.
accepted_findings.fallback: Add clarifying language: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, even if a modification does not materially diminish core functionality, if such modification materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Customer may terminate...' This subordinates the Customer-use test to the core-functionality test but gives Customer an independent termination path.
accepted_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Ambiguous termination-trigger language will be construed against the drafter (Provider) but creates litigation risk and uncertainty for both parties.

rejected_findings.source_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance (which may include cyber liability coverage) with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
rejected_findings.proposed_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than two million U.S. dollars ($2,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate; and (c) cyber liability / technology errors and omissions insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
rejected_findings.external_comment: Given that the Subscription Services are a cloud-hosted SaaS platform processing Customer Data, cyber liability coverage should be a mandatory separate line rather than an optional inclusion within E&O coverage. This ensures adequate protection for data-breach, business-interruption, and technology-failure risks inherent in the service model.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Section 5.3 imposes 72-hour Security Incident notification obligations; Section 5.4 contemplates personal data processing; and the Subscription Services definition confirms cloud-hosted data processing. Without mandatory cyber coverage, Customer bears uninsured risk for data breaches, ransomware, and system failures that E&O policies frequently exclude or sublimit.
rejected_findings.position: Cyber liability insurance as a separate required line at $5M per occurrence and aggregate, distinct from E&O/professional liability at $2M.
rejected_findings.fallback: Cyber liability as a separate required line at $3M per occurrence and aggregate, or cyber coverage as a mandatory endorsement (not optional) to the E&O policy with a sublimit of not less than $3M.
rejected_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. No statutory data-breach insurance mandates beyond general commercial reasonableness for technology service providers.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than two million U.S. dollars ($2,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate; and (c) cyber liability / technology errors and omissions insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: LLM escalation: edit moves contract against client in role.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' + Section 4.3 cure period asymmetry (30 days Customer, 60 days Lattice) + absence of termination for convenience
auditor_findings.external_comment: The combination of (1) no termination-for-convenience right, (2) 100% remaining-fees obligation on any non-breach termination, (3) asymmetric cure periods heavily favoring Provider, and (4) automatic renewal with only 60-day opt-out creates an existential lock-in structure. Customer cannot exit without Provider breach AND successful navigation of asymmetric cure, faces 100% fee liability if exiting for business reasons, and auto-renews into additional lock-in periods. On a three-year $3M Initial Term, this becomes a non-dischargeable $3M obligation with no practical exit except Provider's material breach—which Provider gets 60 days to cure while Customer gets only 30. Individual specialists flagged components but missed the combined existential character: this is economic hostage-taking, not a subscription.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Termination-remedies-analyst flagged findings 002 and 003 as blockers but marked existential:false for both. The COMBINATION of (a) no convenience termination + (b) 100% remaining fees + (c) asymmetric cure creating exit barriers + (d) auto-renewal extending the trap creates EXISTENTIAL lock-in that none of the individual findings captured. A Customer in Year 1 of a 3-year term cannot exit without either (i) proving Provider material breach AND waiting 60 days for cure attempt while paying fees OR (ii) paying 100% of remaining fees ($2M+ on typical enterprise deal). This eliminates all strategic flexibility: acquisition, technology migration, budget reduction, regulatory change, competitive pressure—none permit exit. The auto-renewal in Section 4.2 extends this trap indefinitely unless Customer remembers to opt out 60 days before each renewal. This is not a service agreement—it is financial servitude.
auditor_findings.position: Customer must have termination for convenience after Year 1 with nominal wind-down fee (3 months or less), OR early termination fee capped at 25-50% of remaining fees with immediate effect, OR mutual 45-day cure periods with clear materiality standard.
auditor_findings.fallback: Termination for convenience after 18 months; 50% early termination fee; mutual 45-day cure.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Any structure requiring 100% remaining fees on convenience termination in a multi-year auto-renewing deal with asymmetric cure is existential. Customer cannot accept economic lock-in of this magnitude.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Courts will enforce termination-fee provisions but disfavor penalty clauses and may refuse enforcement where fee + lock-in structure eliminates all practical remedy.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 carve-out (A) 'AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM' + Section 8.6 carve-out (B) unlimited confidentiality breach liability + Section 5.3 Security Incident definition + Section 7.1 Confidential Information includes Customer Data
auditor_findings.external_comment: Risk-allocation-analyst flagged finding 002 (liability cap carve-out scope) and finding 003 (data breach unlimited exposure) separately but missed the COMBINED existential hazard: carve-out (A) 'amounts owed' + carve-out (B) unlimited confidentiality breach creates a two-path structure where ANY claim can be recharacterized to escape the 12-month cap. If Lattice suffers a Security Incident exposing Customer Data, Customer's claim for damages could be framed as either (i) confidentiality breach (unlimited under carve-out B) OR (ii) 'amounts owed' for refund/credit (unlimited under carve-out A). Similarly, Customer's claim for service failures could be recharacterized as 'amounts owed' (unpaid credits, overpaid fees). The 12-month cap becomes illusory—nearly every material claim has a path around it. For a SaaS provider processing customer data, this creates UNINSURABLE unlimited bilateral exposure on every contract.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: The specialists correctly identified that carve-out (A) is problematic and that carve-out (B) creates data-breach exposure, but neither recognized that TOGETHER they eliminate the cap's protective function for BOTH parties. Any payment dispute becomes 'amounts owed' (unlimited). Any data breach becomes confidentiality breach (unlimited). Any warranty claim can be recast as refund demand (amounts owed, unlimited). The interplay makes the stated 12-month cap a nullity in all material dispute scenarios. Combined with Section 9.2 cyber insurance at only $5M, Lattice faces unlimited exposure beyond insurance on every Security Incident, and Customer faces unlimited exposure on every fee dispute recharacterized by Lattice as 'amounts owed.' This is existential for both parties: the risk allocation framework has no effective cap.
auditor_findings.position: Remove carve-out (A) entirely or limit to undisputed unpaid invoices for services actually rendered. Cap carve-out (B) confidentiality breach at $10M super-cap. Add gross negligence/willful misconduct/fraud carve-out as separate item.
auditor_findings.fallback: Cap carve-out (A) at 24 months of fees; cap carve-out (B) at $15M; or cap both at 3x the general cap ($36M on a $1M/year deal).
auditor_findings.walkaway: Unlimited liability on 'amounts owed' for any fee dispute AND unlimited liability for any confidentiality breach simultaneously is existential. At least one must be capped or the general cap becomes meaningless.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Liability caps are enforceable but must actually limit liability; carve-outs that swallow the cap may be recharacterized as illusory and unenforceable.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 10.2 mandatory arbitration in San Francisco + Section 10.2 jury waiver + class action waiver + Section 8.7 consequential damages waiver including 'lost or corrupted data' + Section 2.3 SLA credits as sole remedy + Section 4.4(b) 100% remaining fees on termination
auditor_findings.external_comment: Termination-remedies-analyst flagged findings 004 (arbitration location) and 005 (stacking of waivers) but did not recognize the CROSS-SECTION HAZARD when combined with remedial limitations elsewhere in the contract. The waiver stack (arbitration + jury + class + consequential damages + SLA sole remedy + no data-loss recovery per Section 8.7) eliminates ALL practical remedies for sub-$100K claims, which are the MAJORITY of SaaS disputes (service credits, minor breaches, data-loss incidents, billing disputes). A Customer suffering $50K in losses from service outages must: (1) arbitrate in San Francisco (cost: $30K-80K including travel, filing fees, arbitrator fees, counsel), (2) cannot aggregate with other customers facing same issue (class waiver), (3) cannot recover consequential damages including lost business or corrupted data (Section 8.7), (4) is limited to SLA credits if service-related (Section 2.3), and (5) cannot terminate without paying 100% remaining fees (Section 4.4(b)). The remedy costs exceed the claim value, creating practical immunity for all claims under $100K. Termination-remedies-analyst saw the individual waivers but missed that the COMBINATION with SLA sole remedy + consequential damages waiver + termination fee creates a zone of unrecoverable harm.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: The combination of procedural barriers (San Francisco arbitration, class waiver) + substantive remedy limitations (consequential damages waiver, SLA sole remedy, termination fee) + existential lock-in (findings 001/002) creates three tiers of claim where Customer has no economic remedy: (1) claims under $50K are uneconomical to pursue in arbitration, (2) claims $50K-$200K face consequential damages waiver + sole remedy limitations that cap recovery below arbitration costs, (3) claims over $200K face liability cap (12 months fees) that may be less than damages + arbitration costs on large deals. The waiver stack is not individually existential but COMBINED with the remedial limitations it becomes a practical bar to enforcement for 80%+ of probable disputes. Specialists treated these as separate moderate/major findings; the cross-section makes it a major hazard because it eliminates remedy across the claim spectrum.
auditor_findings.position: Remove class waiver entirely; arbitration in Customer's home state or remote; remove 'lost or corrupted data' from consequential damages waiver; add exception to sole remedy for repeated SLA failures; cap termination fee at 25-50%.
auditor_findings.fallback: Retain class waiver but add small-claims court carve-out for claims under $25K; arbitration in defendant's state; remove 'lost or corrupted data' from consequential waiver; cap termination fee at 50%.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware choice of law; AAA arbitration rules; federal FAA governs arbitration enforceability. Delaware and federal courts enforce waiver stacks but disfavor constructs that eliminate all practical remedy.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.3 IP indemnity uncapped (carve-out C in Section 8.6) + Section 9.2 E&O/cyber insurance $5M + Section 8.6 carve-out structure creating unlimited exposure on multiple paths
auditor_findings.external_comment: Risk-allocation-analyst flagged finding 004 noting uncapped IP indemnity but marked it major/non-existential. Insurance-coverage-analyst flagged finding 001 noting cyber should be separate mandatory coverage but did not connect it to the uncapped indemnity exposure. The CROSS-SECTION HAZARD: Lattice has $5M cyber/E&O insurance (Section 9.2) but UNCAPPED liability for IP infringement (Section 8.3, carve-out C), UNCAPPED liability for confidentiality breach (carve-out B), and potentially uncapped liability for 'amounts owed' (carve-out A). On a patent-troll claim seeking $20M, Lattice's insurance covers $5M and Lattice is personally liable for $15M—potentially exceeding the company's enterprise value. The insurance-to-exposure mismatch is STRUCTURAL: three uncapped carve-outs, one $5M policy, and no discussion of how the gap gets covered. For a SaaS provider, this is uninsurable risk on every customer contract.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Individual specialists noted components but missed the combined exposure: IP indemnity (uncapped) + confidentiality breach (uncapped) + amounts owed (uncapped) + insurance ($5M) = $15M+ uninsured tail risk on EVERY CONTRACT. If Customer is enterprise-scale (Fortune 500), a single IP infringement claim or data breach could bankrupt Lattice. Lattice cannot obtain insurance to cover the gap (no insurer will write unlimited IP or data-breach coverage), cannot pass the cost to Customer (no negotiating leverage on Provider paper), and cannot cap the liability (carve-outs are explicit). The specialists treated these as separate findings; the combination makes it a major cross-section hazard because every uncapped path exceeds available insurance by 2-4x, and all three paths are likely to be invoked in any significant dispute.
auditor_findings.position: Cap IP indemnity at $15M; cap confidentiality breach at $10M; remove 'amounts owed' carve-out; increase cyber insurance to $10M if super-caps adopted.
auditor_findings.fallback: Cap IP indemnity at available E&O insurance ($5M) plus additional $5M layer ($10M total); cap confidentiality breach at $10M; narrow 'amounts owed' to undisputed invoices only.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Uncapped indemnity obligations are enforceable but create uninsurable risk; sophisticated parties typically negotiate super-caps tied to insurance or deal economics.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement, and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.'
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes to add Section 2.3 language permitting termination for repeated SLA failures (three failures in six months) with 30-day notice and 'without liability for future Fees under the affected Order Form.' However, Section 4.4(b) remains unmodified and still requires Customer to 'pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' if Customer terminates other than for Lattice's uncured material breach under Section 4.3. The proposed SLA-based termination right in performance-obligations-analyst-001 is not a Section 4.3 termination for material breach with 60-day cure—it is a new termination mechanism with a three-in-six threshold. As drafted, the two provisions contradict: the new SLA termination right says 'without liability for future Fees' while Section 4.4(b) says Customer pays 100% of remaining Fees for any non-Section-4.3 termination. This creates ambiguity that will be exploited in litigation.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer terminates under the proposed performance-obligations-analyst-001 SLA repeated-failure provision, Provider will argue Section 4.4(b) still applies because the termination was not 'for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3'—it was for repeated SLA failures under a new Section 2.3 carve-out. Customer will argue the 'without liability for future Fees' language in the new SLA provision controls as lex specialis. The contract as signed contains an internal contradiction that makes the new SLA termination right economically unusable if Provider can still invoke Section 4.4(b). This defeats the entire purpose of the accepted finding. The contradiction must be resolved by conforming Section 4.4(b) to exclude SLA-based terminations or by adding explicit cross-reference language.
coherence_findings.position: Modify Section 4.4(b) to add carve-out: 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3 or pursuant to Section 2.3 for repeated SLA failures, Customer shall pay...' OR add to performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposed text: 'For clarity, termination under this paragraph shall not trigger the payment obligations in Section 4.4(b).'
coherence_findings.fallback: Add explicit cross-reference in Section 2.3 new language: 'without liability for future Fees under the affected Order Form, and Section 4.4(b) shall not apply to terminations under this paragraph.'
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Contract interpretation rules favor harmonizing provisions where possible, but where two provisions directly contradict on payment obligation, the ambiguity will be construed against the drafter (Provider), likely in Customer's favor. However, litigation risk and cost make this an unacceptable outcome for both parties.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' AND accepted findings commercial-terms-analyst-006, termination-remedies-analyst-002, termination-remedies-analyst-003 which all modify or interact with Section 4.4(b).
coherence_findings.external_comment: Three accepted findings propose edits to or additions interacting with Section 4.4(b)'s termination-fee structure: commercial-terms-analyst-006 replaces the 100% fee obligation with 50%; termination-remedies-analyst-002 replaces it with 50% or 3 months' Fees whichever is less; termination-remedies-analyst-003 inserts a new Section 4.3A termination-for-convenience right with 25% fee or 2 months' Fees (whichever is less) if terminated in first 12 months, zero fee after 12 months. These three findings propose THREE DIFFERENT termination-fee structures for what appears to be the same triggering event (Customer convenience termination). The contract as signed will contain contradictory fee obligations unless the findings are consolidated or one is designated as controlling.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If all three edits are accepted as written, the signed contract will contain: (1) Section 4.4(b) saying 50% of remaining Fees (commercial-terms-analyst-006), (2) the same Section 4.4(b) also saying 50% or 3 months whichever is less (termination-remedies-analyst-002), and (3) a new Section 4.3A saying 25% or 2 months (whichever is less) if in first 12 months and zero fee thereafter (termination-remedies-analyst-003). Provider will argue the highest fee applies; Customer will argue the lowest. The three provisions are internally contradictory and cannot all be applied simultaneously. This is a redlining error that makes the contract unexecutable as drafted.
coherence_findings.position: Consolidate the three findings into a single termination-fee structure. Recommend adopting termination-remedies-analyst-003 as the most customer-favorable and coherent approach: new Section 4.3A with 25% or 2 months (whichever less) in Year 1, zero thereafter. Delete or mark as superseded commercial-terms-analyst-006 and termination-remedies-analyst-002. If Client insists on retaining fallback options, designate one finding as 'Position' and others as 'Fallback' or 'Alternative,' not as simultaneous edits.
coherence_findings.fallback: Retain termination-remedies-analyst-003 (new Section 4.3A) and modify Section 4.4(b) per termination-remedies-analyst-002 to apply only if Section 4.3A does not apply (i.e., as a backstop). Delete commercial-terms-analyst-006 as duplicative.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Three contradictory fee provisions governing the same event will render the contract ambiguous and likely unenforceable as to the fee term, forcing either settlement negotiation or judicial interpretation against the drafter.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 liability cap carve-outs as proposed by risk-allocation-analyst-002 and risk-allocation-analyst-003, which modify the same sentence with overlapping but non-identical language.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-002 proposes to replace Section 8.6 carve-outs with: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY), AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD'. Accepted finding risk-allocation-analyst-003 proposes to replace the same sentence with: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), UP TO AN AGGREGATE CAP OF TEN MILLION U.S. DOLLARS ($10,000,000); (B) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY); (C) CUSTOMER'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.4A (CUSTOMER INDEMNITY); AND (D) A PARTY'S GROSS NEGLIGENCE, WILLFUL MISCONDUCT, OR FRAUD'. Both findings target the exact same source_text and propose replacement language. The difference is that risk-allocation-analyst-003 adds a $10M super-cap to the confidentiality carve-out while risk-allocation-analyst-002 leaves it uncapped. These are two alternative proposals for the same edit, not two separate edits. As presented, both cannot be applied—only one replacement can be made.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The redline will show two competing replacement texts for Section 8.6 carve-outs, which is nonsensical. The reviewer must choose between uncapped confidentiality breach liability (risk-allocation-analyst-002) or $10M super-cap (risk-allocation-analyst-003). The findings should have been structured as Position/Fallback within a single finding, not as two separate 'replace' markups targeting the same text. This creates ambiguity in the review process and risks the wrong version being accepted if not caught during QA.
coherence_findings.position: Consolidate risk-allocation-analyst-002 and risk-allocation-analyst-003 into a single finding. Adopt risk-allocation-analyst-003 as the Position (includes $10M super-cap on confidentiality breach) and note risk-allocation-analyst-002 as the Fallback (uncapped confidentiality but with gross negligence carve-out). The $10M super-cap is more commercially reasonable and insurable given Provider's $5M cyber insurance.
coherence_findings.fallback: Present both to Client as alternatives with clear recommendation for risk-allocation-analyst-003 due to insurability and coherence with Section 9.2 insurance limits.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. This is a drafting coherence issue, not a legal-interpretation issue.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 2.4: 'Lattice may, in its sole discretion, modify, update, or enhance the Subscription Services from time to time, provided that no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form during the then-current Subscription Term.' AND accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-002 which adds termination right for material adverse changes.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-002 proposes to add language to Section 2.4 giving Customer a termination right with pro-rata refund if a modification 'materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services.' However, the existing Section 2.4 language says 'no such modification will materially diminish the core functionality.' These two standards—'materially diminish core functionality' (Provider's obligation) versus 'materially and adversely affects Customer's use' (Customer's termination trigger)—are overlapping but not identical. A modification could materially and adversely affect Customer's specific use case (e.g., breaks a critical integration Customer relies on) without materially diminishing the core functionality described in the Order Form (e.g., the platform still does the core job for most users). This creates ambiguity: does Customer have a termination right in that scenario or not? Provider will argue no (core functionality not diminished), Customer will argue yes (adverse effect on use). The two standards need to be harmonized or the relationship between them clarified.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: The accepted finding creates a termination right tied to 'material adverse effect on Customer's use' but leaves the existing 'no material diminishment of core functionality' standard in place. These are two different tests applied to the same modification event. If Provider makes a change that satisfies the 'no material diminishment' standard (Provider's view: core functionality intact) but Customer claims 'material adverse effect on use' (Customer's view: my integration broke), the contract does not clearly resolve who is right. The termination right becomes uncertain and likely leads to dispute. The coherence issue is that the new language in performance-obligations-analyst-002 does not integrate with or supersede the existing Section 2.4 standard—it adds a second, overlapping standard without clarifying the relationship.
coherence_findings.position: Modify the proposed performance-obligations-analyst-002 language to clarify: 'If any such modification (a) materially diminishes the core functionality of the Subscription Services described in the applicable Order Form, or (b) materially and adversely affects Customer's ability to use the Subscription Services for the purposes described in the applicable Order Form, Customer may terminate...' This makes clear the termination right applies to EITHER Provider breaching the no-diminishment standard OR a customer-specific material adverse impact.
coherence_findings.fallback: Add clarifying language: 'Notwithstanding the foregoing, even if a modification does not materially diminish core functionality, if such modification materially and adversely affects Customer's use of the Subscription Services, Customer may terminate...' This subordinates the Customer-use test to the core-functionality test but gives Customer an independent termination path.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware. Ambiguous termination-trigger language will be construed against the drafter (Provider) but creates litigation risk and uncertainty for both parties.
